startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: Work through this clinical case to test your understanding of key concepts in General Physiology. Make diagnostic and management decisions based on the clinical scenario.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You made the correct clinical decisions at each step. You demonstrated strong understanding of the underlying concepts. Well done!
You showed some understanding but chose a suboptimal path. Review the feedback provided and try again to reinforce your learning.
Your clinical reasoning needs strengthening in this area. Review the source material carefully, paying attention to the key concepts highlighted, then attempt the case again.

params.text:
Case: Acute Dehydration

A 22-year-old marathon runner collapses after a race on a hot day. He is confused and has dry mucous membranes. His blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg and heart rate is 120 bpm. Laboratory results show serum sodium of 155 mEq/L and serum osmolality of 310 mOsm/kg.

Key concept: Body fluid is distributed between intracellular (approximately two-thirds of total body water) and extracellular (approximately one-third) compartments. Osmosis drives water movement across semipermeable membranes from regions of low solute concentration to high solute concentration.

branchingQuestion.question: What is the primary mechanism causing water to shift between fluid compartments in this patient?

alternatives.text: Osmosis — water moves from low to high solute concentration across a semipermeable membrane

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: Osmosis is the key mechanism governing water distribution between compartments.

alternatives.text: Active transport via the sodium-potassium ATPase pump

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: The Na-K ATPase maintains the resting membrane potential but is not the primary mechanism for bulk water movement between compartments.

alternatives.text: Facilitated diffusion through aquaporin channels only

feedback.title: Incomplete
feedback.subtitle: Aquaporins are water channels, but the driving force for water movement is osmosis — the osmotic gradient determines direction.

params.text:
Investigation Results

IV normal saline is started. After 1 litre, his blood pressure improves to 110/70 mmHg. Repeat labs show sodium 148 mEq/L. His confusion is improving.

Concept: The resting membrane potential of a neuron is approximately -70 mV, maintained by the sodium-potassium ATPase pump which uses ATP to move 3 Na+ out and 2 K+ in against their concentration gradients. Electrolyte disturbances can alter membrane potential and cause neurological symptoms.

branchingQuestion.question: Why did this patient develop confusion with hypernatraemia? What maintains the normal resting membrane potential of neurons?

alternatives.text: The sodium-potassium ATPase pump maintains resting potential at -70 mV; hypernatraemia disrupts this gradient

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: The Na-K ATPase uses ATP to maintain the -70 mV resting potential. Hypernatraemia alters the electrochemical gradient, causing neuronal dysfunction.

alternatives.text: Passive diffusion of chloride ions is the sole determinant of resting potential

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: While chloride contributes, the Na-K ATPase pump is the primary maintainer of resting membrane potential through active transport against concentration gradients.

alternatives.text: Confusion is caused by direct sodium toxicity to neurons, unrelated to membrane potential

feedback.title: Not accurate
feedback.subtitle: The neurological effects are due to osmotic water shifts and altered membrane potentials, not direct sodium toxicity.

params.text:
Clinical Resolution

With gradual rehydration using hypotonic fluids, the patient's sodium normalises over 48 hours. His mental status returns to normal.

Learning points:

  • Active transport requires ATP and moves substances against their concentration gradient
  • The cell membrane's phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins controls selective permeability
  • Osmosis governs water movement between fluid compartments
  • The Na-K ATPase pump is essential for maintaining resting membrane potential
